description: The heavy, downward feeling of loss, disappointment, or grief — named plainly so it can be acknowledged, sat with, and consoled ('phamo'). Phi treats sadness as weather of the heart: real, passing, and speakable without shame.
sound_symbolism: The grounding nasal 'n' sinks into the deepest vowel 'u' — the weight itself, low in the body. Then 'he': an exhale, the breath moving through the heaviness rather than holding it. To say 'nuhe' is to feel the weight and breathe it out in one word.
grammatical_notes: Noun: 'mia nuhe phaelo' (I feel sadness). A counted episode may take the optional classifier of abstractions and events: 'ta nophe nuhe' (one episode of sadness); 'ta nuhe' is also grammatical. Companion words: 'phamo' (console), 'siora' (joy) as its complement, 'therilu' (rest) as its medicine.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The first noble truth speaks plainly of sorrow. Naming sadness without euphemism is the beginning of compassion toward it — feeling acknowledged is feeling already easing.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A speakable sadness needs no aggression to be heard. 'Nuhe' gives grief a gentle word so it need not become a sharp one.

semantic_domains.emotion: The heavy feeling of loss or sorrow
